title: Three euros and ten cents
summary: A Munich bus driver finally asks his oldest passenger why she always pays in coins, never with a card.

body.speaker: Frau Schmitt
body.text: Good morning, Mr Becker.

body.speaker: Mr Becker
body.text: Good morning, Frau Schmitt. Same time, same bus.

body.speaker: Frau Schmitt
body.text: Three euros and ten cents, please.

body.speaker: Mr Becker
body.text: You always have the exact coins. You count for one minute. Every day, every day.

body.speaker: Frau Schmitt
body.text: I know. I am sorry to be slow.

body.speaker: Mr Becker
body.text: No. I am not in a hurry. But I have a question.

body.speaker: Frau Schmitt
body.text: Please.

body.speaker: Mr Becker
body.text: Why do you not use a card? Your daughter can buy you a card.

body.speaker: Frau Schmitt
body.text: She buys me a card. I have a card in my bag.

body.speaker: Mr Becker
body.text: Then why coins?

body.speaker: Frau Schmitt
body.text: Mr Becker, I count the coins because I am afraid I will forget how to count.

body.speaker: Mr Becker
body.text: Forget how to count?

body.speaker: Frau Schmitt
body.text: My doctor says my memory is getting smaller. A little every week. The coins help me. Three euros — one, two, three. And ten cents — five, and five. The card is too easy. The card is one second.

body.speaker: Mr Becker
body.text: I understand.

body.speaker: Frau Schmitt
body.text: I count on this bus every day. You see me. I see you. The day I cannot count the coins, the day is here. I will tell you. I will use the card.

body.speaker: Mr Becker
body.text: Don't be sad for me, Frau Schmitt.

body.speaker: Frau Schmitt
body.text: I am not sad. I am here.

body.speaker: Mr Becker
body.text: Take your time. The bus waits.

body.speaker: Frau Schmitt
body.text: Thank you, Mr Becker.

body.speaker: Mr Becker
body.text: One, two, three… and ten cents.

body.speaker: Frau Schmitt
body.text: Today is a good day.

annotations.meaningVi: chính xác (đúng số, không hơn không kém)
cứ từ từ / không cần vội (cụm cố định)
ngày càng nhỏ lại / suy giảm dần (diễn tả quá trình liên tục)
xin lỗi vì chậm (mẫu câu xin lỗi: sorry + to + động từ nguyên thể)
vẫn giờ đó, vẫn chuyến xe đó (cách nói lặp lại để nhấn mạnh thói quen)
tôi hiểu rồi (cụm phản hồi thông dụng)

grammarNotes.title: Giving reasons with 'because'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Để giải thích lý do, A1 dùng từ nối 'because + mệnh đề'. 'I count the coins because I am afraid I will forget how to count.' Vế sau 'because' là một câu đầy đủ (chủ ngữ + động từ), không phải chỉ một từ. So sánh: 'Why do you not use a card?' (câu hỏi) → 'Because I am afraid…' (câu trả lời).
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Use 'because + clause' to give a reason. 'I count the coins because I am afraid I will forget how to count.' The part after 'because' must be a full clause with a subject and a verb. Answer 'Why…?' questions with 'Because…'.

translationVi:
Bà Schmitt: Chào ông Becker.
Ông Becker: Chào bà Schmitt. Vẫn giờ này, vẫn chuyến xe này.
Bà Schmitt: Ba euro mười xu, làm ơn.
Ông Becker: Bà luôn có đủ tiền xu chính xác. Bà đếm mất một phút. Ngày nào cũng vậy, ngày nào cũng vậy.
Bà Schmitt: Tôi biết. Tôi xin lỗi vì chậm.
Ông Becker: Không. Tôi không vội đâu. Nhưng tôi có một câu hỏi.
Bà Schmitt: Ông hỏi đi.
Ông Becker: Sao bà không dùng thẻ? Con gái bà mua thẻ cho bà được mà.
Bà Schmitt: Nó có mua cho tôi. Tôi có thẻ trong túi.
Ông Becker: Vậy sao bà dùng xu?
Bà Schmitt: Ông Becker à, tôi đếm xu vì tôi sợ tôi sẽ quên cách đếm.
Ông Becker: Quên cách đếm?
Bà Schmitt: Bác sĩ nói trí nhớ tôi nhỏ dần. Mỗi tuần một chút. Mấy đồng xu giúp tôi. Ba euro — một, hai, ba. Và mười xu — năm và năm. Thẻ thì dễ quá. Thẻ là một giây.
Ông Becker: Tôi hiểu rồi.
Bà Schmitt: Mỗi ngày tôi đếm trên xe này. Ông thấy tôi. Tôi thấy ông. Ngày nào tôi không đếm được xu nữa, ngày đó đến rồi. Tôi sẽ nói với ông. Tôi sẽ dùng thẻ.
Ông Becker: Bà đừng buồn cho bản thân, bà Schmitt.
Bà Schmitt: Tôi không buồn. Tôi đang ở đây.
Ông Becker: Bà cứ từ từ. Xe đợi được.
Bà Schmitt: Cảm ơn ông Becker.
Ông Becker: Một, hai, ba… và mười xu.
Bà Schmitt: Hôm nay là một ngày tốt.

questions.explanation: Mr Becker is the driver; Frau Schmitt is the passenger.
questions.hint: Look at who collects the money.

questions.prompt: How much is the bus fare?
questions.hint: Frau Schmitt says it at the start.

questions.prompt: Does Frau Schmitt have a bus card?
options: Yes, her daughter bought her one, and it is in her bag.
questions.explanation: 'She buys me a card. I have a card in my bag.'
questions.hint: Look at what Frau Schmitt says about the card.

questions.prompt: Why does Frau Schmitt pay with coins?
options: She is afraid she will forget how to count.
She does not trust the daughter.
questions.explanation: 'I count the coins because I am afraid I will forget how to count.'

options: Her memory is getting smaller, a little every week.
questions.explanation: 'My doctor says my memory is getting smaller. A little every week.'

questions.prompt: Why is the card 'too easy' for her?
options: The card is only one second — there is no counting practice.
questions.explanation: 'The card is too easy. The card is one second.'
questions.hint: She compares one second with the time it takes to count coins.

questions.prompt: When will Frau Schmitt start using the card?
options: On the day she cannot count the coins anymore
questions.explanation: 'The day I cannot count the coins, the day is here. I will tell you. I will use the card.'
questions.hint: Look at what she says about a future day.

questions.prompt: How does Mr Becker react to her story?
options: He says he understands and tells her to take her time.
questions.explanation: 'I understand.' / 'Take your time. The bus waits.'
questions.hint: Look at Mr Becker's two key lines after he hears the reason.

questions.prompt: What does Frau Schmitt mean by 'Today is a good day'?
options: She counted the coins correctly today — her memory still works.
She is travelling to a new city.
questions.explanation: She has just successfully counted out the exact fare with Mr Becker watching.
questions.hint: Look at the line right before — Mr Becker counts the coins out loud.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the dialogue?
options: A small daily habit can be one person's quiet way of staying themselves.
Old people should stop taking the bus.
questions.explanation: Counting coins is Frau Schmitt's way of protecting her memory and her independence.
questions.hint: Think about what the coins do for Frau Schmitt that the card cannot.

cloze.template:
Frau Schmitt: Good morning, Mr Becker.
Mr Becker: Good morning, Frau Schmitt. Same time, same {{b1}}.
Frau Schmitt: Three euros and ten cents, please.
Mr Becker: You always have the exact {{b2}}. You count for one minute. Every day, every day.
Frau Schmitt: I know. I am sorry to be slow.
Mr Becker: No. I am not in a {{b3}}. But I have a question.
Frau Schmitt: Please.
Mr Becker: Why do you not use a {{b4}}? Your daughter can buy you a card.
Frau Schmitt: She buys me a card. I have a card in my bag.
Mr Becker: Then why coins?
Frau Schmitt: Mr Becker, I count the coins because I am {{b5}} I will forget how to count.
Mr Becker: Forget how to count?
Frau Schmitt: My doctor says my {{b6}} is getting smaller. A little every week. The coins help me. Three euros — one, two, three. And ten cents — five, and five. The card is too {{b7}}. The card is one second.
Mr Becker: I understand.
Frau Schmitt: I count on this bus every day. You see me. I see you. The day I cannot count the coins, the day is here. I will tell you. I will use the card.
Mr Becker: Don't be sad for me, Frau Schmitt.
Frau Schmitt: I am not sad. I am here.
Mr Becker: Take your time. The bus {{b8}}.
Frau Schmitt: Thank you, Mr Becker.
Mr Becker: One, two, three… and ten {{b9}}.
Frau Schmitt: Today is a {{b10}} day.

blanks.explanation: She always has the exact coins.
He asks why she does not use a card.
She is afraid she will forget how to count.
The card is too easy — no practice.
She says it is a good day — she counted correctly.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Frau Schmitt chooses the slower way to pay because the harder thing protects something she does not want to lose. Is there something in your life you do the slow, hard way on purpose? What would the easy way cost you?
